task: I’ve got a unit where the forecast is making me nervous about possible damage, and I want to think through the smartest way to protect myself before anything happens. What should I check or put in place now so I’m not scrambling later?

turns.agent_response:
Before the weather hits, I’d set this up like a liability/claims checklist so you’re not reacting later.

### 1) Document the unit now
- Take date-stamped photos/video of:
  - ceilings, walls, windows, exterior envelope, roof access points
  - under sinks, around water heaters/HVAC, electrical panels
  - any existing stains, cracks, soft spots, or prior patchwork
- Note current condition in writing, especially anything already questionable.
- If there’s any known prior damage, make sure it’s documented separately so it can’t get blended into a new claim.

### 2) Verify building systems and likely failure points
Check for:
- active leaks or prior leak history
- roof/drainage issues
- clogged gutters/scuppers
- window/door sealing problems
- sump pumps/backflow protection if applicable
- exposed or vulnerable plumbing
- HVAC condensate issues
- electrical panels or equipment in areas that could be impacted

If there’s any hint of unpermitted or noncompliant work in those areas, verify before touching it.

### 3) Confirm insurance and coverage details now
Pull the policy and verify:
- wind, hail, flood, sewer backup, and water intrusion coverage
- deductibles, especially named-storm or hurricane deductibles
- exclusions for neglect, pre-existing damage, or maintenance issues
- claim reporting deadlines
- whether temporary repairs are covered
- requirements for photo evidence, invoices, and mitigation steps

Also check if there were prior claims on this unit/building that could complicate a new one.

### 4) Line up mitigation supplies and vendors
Have ready:
- tarps, plastic, tape, buckets, towels, wet/dry vac
- dehumidifier/fans if water intrusion is likely
- contact info for roofer, plumber, restoration company, electrician
- emergency board-up or tarp vendor if that’s relevant
- someone who can physically check the property after the event

### 5) Reduce exposure before the event
- Clear drains/gutters
- Secure loose exterior items
- Shut off or isolate anything unnecessary if the unit will be vacant
- Move valuables or vulnerable items away from windows/exterior walls
- Make sure the unit is accessible for post-storm inspection
- If there’s a known leak risk, consider shutting off water to the unit if occupancy allows

### 6) Set your post-event process now
Decide in advance:
- who inspects first
- who takes photos
- who is authorized to make temporary repairs
- what gets reported to insurance and when
- what threshold triggers a contractor call

That way you’re not making rushed calls after the fact.

### 7) Keep a clean paper trail
Save:
- weather alerts
- inspection notes
- photos/videos
- vendor communications
- receipts for any mitigation work
- tenant notices if applicable

### 8) If tenants are involved
- Send a calm heads-up about reporting leaks, power issues, or damage immediately
- Ask them not to perform repairs themselves
- Get a clear contact path for emergencies

If you want, I can turn this into a **storm-prep cut sheet** you can use for the unit, with checkboxes for **insurance, maintenance, documentation, and post-event response**.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good, but I’d tighten it into an actual pre-storm control list. You missed a few things that matter if I’m trying not to eat a loss later:

- confirm whether the unit is occupied or vacant, because that changes what I can shut off and who I need to notify
- check the HOA/association rules if there are any, especially on exterior work, access, and emergency mitigation
- verify the water shutoff location and whether it actually works
- if there’s a roof or upper-floor risk, get a handyman or roofer eyes-on before the weather, not after
- make sure the insurance carrier has the right loss notice contact and claim number process, not just a generic policy copy
- if I’m expecting wind-driven rain, I want window/door sealing checked, not just “documented”

I’d also want this organized by: 1) before the storm, 2) during, 3) after. Right now it’s useful, but still a little generic. Give me the version I can actually hand to a manager or onsite person.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter version, organized the way you asked and written like a control list someone onsite can actually run.

---

# Pre-Storm Control List
## 1) Before the storm

### Occupancy / access
- Confirm whether the unit is **occupied or vacant**
- If occupied:
  - notify tenant of weather risk and emergency contact process
  - confirm tenant knows where to report leaks, power loss, or damage
  - tell tenant not to make repairs unless authorized
- If vacant:
  - confirm who has keys/access
  - confirm who can enter after the storm
  - consider extra shutoff/protection steps if the unit will sit empty

### HOA / association
- Check whether there is an **HOA/COA/association**
- Confirm rules on:
  - exterior work
  - roof access
  - emergency mitigation
  - board-up/tarping
  - vendor access and notice requirements
- If there’s a management company, get the correct emergency contact now

### Water shutoff
- Locate the **main water shutoff**
- Test that it actually works
- Verify:
  - unit shutoff vs building shutoff
  - where the valve is
  - who is allowed to operate it
- If there’s known leak risk and occupancy allows it, shut off water before the event

### Roof / upper-floor / exterior risk
- If there is roof exposure, upper-floor exposure, or known leak history:
  - get a **handyman, roofer, or property pro to eyes-on it before the storm**
  - check flashing, penetrations, loose shingles, drainage, gutters, scuppers, and obvious weak points
- Don’t wait to inspect after damage shows up

### Windows / doors / wind-driven rain
- Check window and door sealing
- Look for:
  - failed caulk
  - broken seals
  - gaps
  - loose frames
  - damaged thresholds
  - missing weatherstripping
- If wind-driven rain is likely, verify closures and sealing now

### Insurance / claim setup
- Confirm the policy is current and active
- Verify:
  - loss notice contact
  - claim reporting process
  - claim number process, if one is needed
  - emergency/after-hours contact
  - deductible type and amount
  - storm, wind, hail, flood, sewer backup, and water intrusion coverage
- Save the carrier’s actual claim reporting method, not just the policy copy
- If there have been prior claims, keep those separate and documented

### Documentation
- Take date-stamped photos/video of:
  - ceilings, walls, floors, windows, doors
  - roof access areas
  - under sinks
  - water heater, HVAC, electrical panel
  - any existing stains, cracks, soft spots, or patchwork
- Note pre-existing conditions in writing
- Save everything in one folder before the event

### Mitigation prep
- Stage supplies:
  - tarps
  - plastic sheeting
  - tape
  - buckets
  - towels
  - wet/dry vac
  - fans/dehumidifier if needed
- Line up vendors:
  - roofer
  - plumber
  - restoration company
  - handyman
  - electrician if needed
- Confirm who can do emergency mitigation and who can authorize it

### Site protection
- Clear loose exterior items
- Secure anything that can blow around
- Make sure drains/gutters/scuppers are clear
- Move vulnerable items away from exterior walls/windows if needed
- Confirm the unit is physically accessible after the storm

---

## 2) During the storm

### Safety first
- No unnecessary site entry
- Don’t send anyone out if conditions are unsafe
- If the building has active flooding, electrical risk, or structural concern, treat it as a safety issue first

### Monitor and respond
- Watch for:
  - water intrusion
  - power loss
  - roof damage
  - broken windows/doors
  - standing water
- If a leak or breach is reported:
  - document it immediately
  - notify the right person
  - authorize only temporary mitigation if safe

### Tenant communication
- If occupied, remind tenants:
  - report leaks immediately
  - do not use damaged electrical equipment
  - do not start repairs on their own
  - keep photos if safe to do so

---

## 3) After the storm

### First inspection
- Inspect as soon as it is safe
- Check:
  - roof
  - windows/doors
  - ceilings/walls
  - floors/baseboards
  - electrical panel
  - HVAC
  - plumbing
  - signs of intrusion, staining, or saturation

### Document everything
- Take new date-stamped photos/video
- Compare against pre-storm condition
- Separate:
  - new damage
  - old damage
  - temporary mitigation work

### Immediate mitigation
- Stop active leaks if possible
- Dry the area
- Remove standing water
- Tarp or board up if needed
- Call the right vendor if the damage is beyond basic containment

### Insurance notice
- Report the loss promptly through the carrier’s actual process
- Use the correct claim contact / reporting method
- Keep the claim number, contact name, and timestamp of notice
- Save all receipts and vendor invoices

### Follow-up
- Track:
  - estimates
  - scope of damage
  - repairs authorized
  - tenant notices
  - HOA communications
  - insurance correspondence
- Don’t let temporary repairs get mixed up with permanent work

---

# Simple handoff version for onsite use

## Before
- Confirm occupied or vacant
- Notify tenant or manager
- Check HOA rules and contacts
- Locate and test water shutoff
- Get roof/exterior eyes-on if risk exists
- Check window/door sealing
- Confirm insurance claim contact and reporting process
- Photograph current condition
- Stage mitigation supplies
- Line up vendors

## During
- No unsafe access
- Monitor for leaks, power loss, broken openings
- Document issues as reported
- Authorize only emergency mitigation
- Keep tenant communication tight

## After
- Inspect safely
- Photograph all damage
- Compare to pre-storm condition
- Stop active leaks / dry out / tarp or board up
- Report claim through carrier process
- Save receipts and vendor records

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checkbox sheet** with blanks for:
- unit address
- occupied/vacant
- HOA contact
- shutoff location
- carrier claim number
- vendor names
- post-storm inspection notes

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the version I needed. Clean enough to hand off, and it actually separates the decision points instead of mushing everything together.

The only thing I’d add is a line for who has authority to spend money on emergency mitigation without waiting for me — because if the roof is leaking at 9 p.m., I don’t want some onsite person freezing up over a $300 tarp. But otherwise, this is solid.
